segments.s735-g145: This is a prepositional list introduced by the poetic preposition 'ز' (from). The coordinating 'و' links the place/people names in a simple enumerative sequence (Hital, Turk(s), Samarkand, Chach). Word order is the usual Persian noun coordination (Noun + و + Noun).
segments.s735-g146: This noun phrase names the elites: 'بزرگان' (great ones) followed by instruments/attributes introduced with 'با' (with). The sequence 'با فر و اورند وتاج' lists three attributes joined by 'و' — here 'فر' is the mythic royal glory (an abstract noun), 'اورند' is treated as a listed item, and 'وتاج' (and crown) completes the list. The structure is noun + prepositional phrase (با + attributes).
segments.s735-g147: 'همه' (all/everyone) is the quantifier placed before the following noun phrase; 'کهتران' here functions as the group being quantified (contextually 'the lesser/younger ones' or 'inferiors'), 'شما' is the possessor/agent 'your', and 'بوده‌اند' is the compound past (perfect) auxiliary meaning 'have been'. The verb comes at the end in typical Persian order (SOV).
segments.s735-g148: 'برین' is a fused locative/ demonstrative ('بر' + 'این' = 'on/at this'); 'بندگی' is the noun 'servitude/devotion' governed by it. The following 'بر گوا بوده‌اند' places 'گوا' (witness) after a secondary 'بر' (on/for) and closes with the past auxiliary 'بوده‌اند' — overall: 'they have been witnesses to/on this servitude.' The doubled 'بر' marks relation and then the predicative role 'witness'.
segments.s735-g149: The clause begins with the relative/conjunction 'که' (that), followed by the subject 'شاهان' (kings). 'ز تخم فریدون' is a genitive phrase 'from the seed/lineage of Fereydun' (ز + noun), giving the kings' descent. The verb 'بدند' is an archaic/persianized past form meaning 'they were' (past), placed at the end as expected.
segments.s735-g150: 'دگر یکسر' is a phrase meaning 'henceforth/entirely' (دگر = now/anymore/other, یکسر = wholly/altogether). 'از داد بیرون' literally 'out of justice' (از + داد + بیرون) describes their state as being outside or devoid of justice. The clause ends with the same past verb 'بدند' (they were) completing the predicate.
segments.s735-g151: The adverbial 'بدین' (here written as 'ب' + 'دین') means 'by/with this' or 'in this way'; 'خویشی' is the noun 'kinship/relationship'. 'اکنون' ('now') sets the temporal frame and the relative 'که' introduces the clause 'that I have done' with 'من' (I) as subject and 'کرده‌ام' (I have done) as present perfect auxiliary, placed at the end.
segments.s735-g152: 'بزرگی' (greatness) is the direct object; 'به دانش' (by/through knowledge) is an instrumental prepositional phrase indicating means; 'برآورده‌ام' is a compound verb in the perfect ('I have brought/raised/achieved'), with the verb-final position marking the main predicate 'I have brought about greatness by learning.'
segments.s735-g153: بدان گونه شادم که تشنه بر آب is a clause of result/comparison: بدَان گونه (بد + آن گونه) = “in that manner/so” (an adverbial phrase) precedes the copular adjective شادم = “I am happy.” که introduces the result clause “that I am تشنه بر آب” where تشنه is adjectival (“thirsty”) and بر آب uses بر + noun to mean “for/toward water” (i.e. ‘thirsting for water’). Word order is emphatic/poetic (adverbial phrase → predicate → complement clause).
segments.s735-g154: وگر سبزه تیره بر آفتاب is appended as a conditional/contrastive phrase: وگر (و + گر) = “and if / even if,” followed by سبزه تیره (lit. ‘green growth dark’) — adjective after noun as usual — and بر آفتاب = “upon/under the sun.” The clause is elliptical (no finite verb), typical of poetry: the verb is implied from the previous clause.
segments.s735-g155: جهاندار بیدار فرخ کناد is an optative/wish construction: جهاندار = “the sovereign,” بیدار = attributive adjective “watchful/awake,” فرخ = “good-fortune/goodness,” and کناد is an archaic 3rd-person optative/subjunctive (from کردن) meaning “may he do/grant.” The sequence is noun + modifiers + verbal wish, a common Persian order in poetry.
segments.s735-g156: مرا اندرین روز پاسخ کناد continues the optative: مرا = the speaker as object (“me”), indicated here in the text by two split tokens (م + را), اندرین روز = “on/within this day” (اندرین = archaic ‘in this’ + روز = day), پاسخ = “answer,” and again کناد is the optative ‘may he give.’ The object marker را attaches to indicate that مرا is the patient of the hoped-for action.
segments.s735-g157: یکی آرزو خواهم از شهریار is a simple clause with subject omitted (first person): یکی آرزو = ‘one wish/request,’ خواهم is the present/future/modal first-person form of خواستن (‘I will want / I will ask’), and از شهریار = ‘from the king.’ Word order is object/topic → verb → source, idiomatic for Persian ('I will ask one thing of the king').
segments.s735-g158: کجا آن سخن نزد او هست خوار is a rhetorical interrogative: کجا = ‘where/how (can it be),’ آن سخن = ‘that speech/utterance,’ نزد او = ‘in his presence’ (نزد = ‘near, before, in the presence of’), هست = copula ‘is,’ and خوار = predicative adjective ‘despised/held cheap.’ The clause asks rhetorically where or how such a thing could be despised before him.
segments.s735-g159: که دار مسیحا به گنج شماست begins with the subordinating/causal که = ‘for/that,’ followed by دار = ‘abode/house,’ مسیحا = ‘the Messiah’ (subject), به گنج شما = ‘in/among your treasure/treasury’ (به + noun phrase), and ست = poetic/copular form of است ‘is.’ The clause gives the reason or ground for the preceding rhetorical question.
segments.s735-g160: چو بینید دانید گفتار راست is a temporal/result clause: چو = ‘when,’ بینید = 2nd-person plural present subj./imperative ‘you see,’ دانید = 2nd-person plural present/future ‘you will know,’ and گفتار راست = ‘the true/true statement’ (noun + adjective). The two verbs are consecutive actions: when you see (it), you will know the truth of the saying.
segments.s735-g89: This phrase names the speaker and the addressee with a slightly inverted word order: a personal name or phrase (بخراد برزین) followed by چنیـن گفت شاه, a formula meaning ‘thus said (to) the king’. چنیـن is an adverb ‘thus’; گفت is the reporting verb (here 'said' — poetic diction), and شاه is the indirect object/target ('the king').
segments.s735-g90: This is a subordinate content clause introduced by که (= 'that' / 'that this'), giving the command: این نامه ('this letter') is the object and برخوان is a verbal/composite expression meaning 'be read aloud / have read' (here used transitively), followed by به پیش سپاه 'in the presence of the army' where به پیش = 'before / in the presence of' and سپاه = 'army'.
segments.s735-g91: به عنوان is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the post, in the capacity of' (here 'to the office/title'); نگه کرد is a past-tense compound meaning 'kept/appointed' (نگه 'keeping' + کرد 'did/made'); مرد دبیر is the direct object 'a man, a scribe' (مرد modifies دبیر). The whole segment states that someone was retained in an official post.
segments.s735-g92: که introduces a relative clause modifying مرد دبیر ('who/that'). گوینده‌ای is indefinite with the ezafe-like suffix -ای meaning 'a speaker/announcer'; بود is the past copula 'was'; و هم links the next predicate with 'and also'; یادگیر is a noun 'memorizer/learner'. The clause describes the scribe's functions.
segments.s735-g93: چنین گفت is the formula 'thus he said'; کاین is the fused classical form of 'که این' meaning 'that this' / 'this that'; نامه is 'letter'; سوی here functions as 'to/toward' meaning 'addressed to'; مهست is an adjectival/poetic predicate 'is moonlike / beautiful' (here attributing 'moonlike, beautiful' to the addressee). The clause therefore states the intended recipient of the letter.
segments.s735-g94: A nominal phrase listing the addressee's titles/names in apposition: جهاندار (sovereign) + پرویز (the personal name) + یزدان‌پرست (a descriptive epithet 'God-worshipper' / 'pious'). There is no verb here; this names the person to whom the letter is addressed.
segments.s735-g95: A coordinated series of epithets joined by و: جهاندار 'sovereign', بیدار 'alert/vigilant', and پدرام (a proper name). شهر follows as a title or appositive ('of the city' / 'city's'), so the whole sequence functions as a list of titles and a name describing the same person.
segments.s735-g96: که introduces a relative clause about the named ruler. یزدانش is a possessive construction 'his God' (God as subject), تاج و خرد are the direct objects 'crown and wisdom', داد is the past verb 'gave', and بهر is the preposition 'for/to' (here indicating benefit; the explicit pronoun 'him' is omitted but understood). The clause says that his God granted him crown and wisdom.
segments.s735-g201: Two past actions are placed in sequence: بیامد (past of آمدن) ‘he came’ followed by بدید (past of دیدن) ‘he saw’. The subject is omitted (third person singular). آن is a demonstrative determiner 'that' and گزین modifies جایگاه as 'chosen/selected seat/place' (an adjectival use of گزین). Word order (verb–verb–object) is a poetic sequence meaning 'he came and saw that seat.'
segments.s735-g202: وزان پس همی‌بود is a temporal/result clause: وزان (از + آن) 'from that' (i.e. from that time), پس 'then/thereafter', همی‌بود is the imperfective/past-continuous sense 'he remained' or 'he was in the habit of being'. نزدیک شاه places his location 'near the king'. Together: 'from that time on he remained near the king.'
segments.s735-g203: This is a rhythmic list of courtly activities expressed mostly with verbal nouns: بخوان here functions as 'song/singing/recital' (a nominalized verb), نبید is the poetic word for 'wine', شکار 'the hunt', and نشست 'a sitting/session or social gathering'. The coordinating و links the items.
segments.s735-g204: همی‌بود is again 'he used to be / continued to be'. با شاه indicates accompaniment 'with the king'. مهتر پرست is a compound-like description: مهتر 'the great/chief' + پرست 'worshipper/reverer', i.e. 'reverent toward the great' or 'respectful to superiors' — it characterizes his attitude in the king's company. Word order places the descriptive phrase after با شاه as a short appositive description of his behaviour.
segments.s735-g205: برین گونه is a fixed phrase meaning 'in this manner' (بر + این with گونه 'manner'). یک ماه gives the duration 'one month'. نزدیک شاه marks the location 'near the king'. The whole phrase sets time, manner and place: 'in this way for a month near the king...'.
segments.s735-g206: همی‌بود again means 'he continued to be / he would be'. شادان دل is a compound adjective 'joyful-hearted' (شادان modifying دل), and نیک‌خواه is another adjectival/nominal description 'well-wisher' or 'benevolent / well-disposed'. The two qualities are linked by و, describing his state while near the king.
segments.s735-g9: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when' introducing a temporal clause; the clause places the time before the main verbs. آن نامه is the noun phrase 'that letter' and نزدیک قیصر is a locative/relational phrase meaning 'near Caesar' (نزدیک follows the noun it qualifies). رسید is a simple past intransitive verb 'arrived/reached' and completes the temporal clause.
segments.s735-g10: This is a sequence of two simple past actions with an implicit subject ('he'). نگه کرد is a compound-like expression (نگه + کرد) meaning 'he looked/paused his gaze' (کرد as the past tense of کردن makes نگه a verbal noun into an action). و links the two actions. توقیع پرویز is a genitive construction 'the decree/signature of Parviz' and دید is the past of دیدن 'he saw', completing the clause.
segments.s735-g11: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a subordinate clause of content/purpose 'that/so that'. گاو is the plural/collective agent 'cows' and دم here denotes a blowing/breathing sound; بر درش is a locative phrase 'at/against his gate' (بر + در + possessive -ش). The main verb of this subordinate clause appears after it (see next segment).
segments.s735-g12: دمیدند is a past plural verb 'they blew' (agreeing with گاو as the subject of the subordinate clause). و links the next clause. پر بانگ is an adjectival phrase 'full of sound/voices' with پر before بانگ intensifying it. شد is the past auxiliary 'became', and کشورش 'his country/realm' is the subject/experiencer that becomes filled with sound.
segments.s735-g13: ببستند is a past plural 'they closed/ended' (here meaning 'they concluded' or 'they brought to a close'). آیین 'rite/ceremony' is the object. به introduces the domains/locations 'on/at' and the paired phrase بی‌راه و راه contrasts off-road/byway (بی‌راه) with the main road (راه); here the pair describes where the rites were carried out or ended.
segments.s735-g14: پر آواز is a fronted adjectival/epithet 'full-voiced' or 'resounding' that modifies the following name phrase. شیروی is an epithet 'lion-like/heroic' attached to پرویز (the king's name), and شاه is the title 'king'. Together this phrase characterizes Parviz as the lion-hearted king whose name was cried aloud; word order places the descriptive adjectives before the name.
segments.s735-g15: برآمد is a past intransitive 'arose/issued forth'. هم‌آواز is an adverbial/compound meaning 'in unison' or 'in chorus' and directly modifies رامشگران 'peacemakers/comforters', so the whole phrase conveys 'a chorus of peacemakers rose up together.' The verb precedes the noun phrase here, a common poetic word order.
segments.s735-g16: همه شهر روم is a noun phrase 'the whole/city of Rome' (همه as 'whole/entire' modifies شهر). از کران تا کران is a fixed prepositional idiom 'from edge to edge' (از... تا... = from... to...), emphasizing extent 'from one end to the other.' Together they indicate the action extended throughout the entire city.
segments.s735-g113: نه introduces negation for the comparative clauses; چون تو is a simile 'like you' with the copula verb omitted (classical Persian often drops 'است'), so the clause reads literally 'Not like you (is) autumn, and not like you (is) spring.' The second half repeats the pattern with و linking the two negative comparisons.
segments.s735-g114: Again begun by نه + چون تو = 'not like you', followed by the noun phrase بایوان چین بر نگار. چین بر نگار is a prepositional phrase 'fold(s)/wrinkle(s) upon the beloved' (چین + بر + نگار), and بایوان is an archaic/uncertain modifier attached to چین; the verb 'is' is omitted by ellipsis as in the previous clause.
segments.s735-g115: همه is a universal quantifier 'all/every', here modifying مردمی ('of the people') and, after و, another parallel phrase همه راستی ('all truth/uprightness'). The two phrases are coordinated; they set up what follows (they are the agents/causes in the optative construction that follows).
segments.s735-g116: مبیناد is a third-person present-form used optatively/impersonally here ('may it show/reveal' or 'let it not be shown' by context); جانت 'your life/ soul' is the affected possessee (jān + pronominal suffix), بد modifies کاستی 'bad/ill deficiency' (the thing that might be shown). The clause lacks an explicit negative particle, but idiomatically the wish is that no 'بد کاستی' be shown in your soul.
segments.s735-g117: به introduces a list of places, here meaning 'in/through/to' (classical usage can mark domain or range). The three place-nouns are simply coordinated by و; the phrase names the geographical extent or audience under discussion.
segments.s735-g118: همان 'that/the same' points back to the person just mentioned; ترک is 'Turk' (the noun). تا here marks extent 'as far as' or 'up to', so تا روم و جادوستان means 'as far as Rome and Jādūstān (the land of magic/sorcery)'; the clause thus gives the geographic reach associated with that Turk.
segments.s735-g119: تو را is the patient/object 'you' marked with the object marker را; داد is the past tense 'gave'; یزدان follows the verb as the agent (classical VSO or V‑O‑S ordering is common in Ferdowsi). به پاکی نژاد is a prepositional phrase 'with/by/into purity of lineage' (به + abstract noun), indicating what was given.
segments.s735-g120: کسی is an indefinite pronoun 'someone/anyone' (here the context renders it effectively 'no one' or 'none like you'); چون تو is the simile 'like you'. از introduces the source 'from/of', پاک modifies مادر 'pure mother', and نزاد is an archaic past form from زادن used impersonally/passively here 'was born' (third person singular); the subject is omitted because the focus is on the existence/uniqueness of such a birth.
segments.s735-g97: A nominal phrase in apposition: جهاندار (a title) is immediately followed by a genitive/possessive phrase فرزند هرمزد شاه, literally "(the) child/son of Hormozd the king." Persian allows the title and the relationship to stand next to one another without a copula; the sequence names the person (title → kinship → personal name → title).
segments.s735-g98: A relative clause introduced by که ("who/that") modifying the preceding noun phrase. زیبای + noun is a poetic attributive construction meaning "the beauty/ornament of X;" است is the present copula "is." The clause repeats the pattern (زیبای تاج است و زیبای گاه) to assert two parallel predicates joined by و (and).
segments.s735-g99: A compressed origin statement using the preposition ز (from). The sequence uses a paratactic listing: ز قیصر پدر مادر شیر نام should be read as indicating origin/lineage — "from Caesar (as) father; (a) mother called Shir." Persian poetry often omits copulas and particles, so noun chains convey relationships by position.
segments.s735-g100: An optative/wish clause introduced by که plus the optative particle بادا. پاینده is an adjective "everlasting/long-lived" placed before بادا in the fixed formula پاینده بادا = "may ... be everlasting." بدو is the dative/clitic phrase "to him; for him," and نام و کام are the two nouns receiving the wish ("name and desire/fulfillment").
segments.s735-g101: A triadic optative phrase ending in the optative particle باد (here "may it be / may he be"). The three desiderata are coordinated: (ابا ?)فر, و با برز, و پیروز — each element expressed as "with X" (با) or as the noun X itself (فر). This is a compact poetic wish: "may he be endowed with fravashi/royal radiance, with exalted standing, and with victory." The initial token ابا is anomalous/poetic here (see senses); grammatically the line functions by enumerating qualities followed by باد to form the optative.
segments.s735-g102: Another optative clause: همه روزگارانش ("all his days/ages") is the noun phrase receiving the following wish نوروز باد ("may it be Nowruz"). This is an idiomatic blessing meaning "may every one of his days be as joyful/renewed as Nowruz." باد is again the optative particle.
segments.s735-g103: A goal/benefactive phrase introduced by به ("to/for"): به ایران و تورانش names the domains (Iran and Turan — تورانش here with the third‑person possessive), then بر دست رس is a verbal phrase meaning "reach into (his) hand" or idiomatically "come into (his) power." Persian orders the prepositional phrase for the recipient first, then the verbal phrase indicating attainment.
segments.s735-g104: A negative-optative clause: به شاهی ("to kingship/kingship") is the thing wished about; مباداش (a poetic/older form of مبادا) expresses "may it not be" or "may there not be" (i.e., let there not be...). It is followed by the noun phrase انباز کس — an agent phrase meaning something like "a person who opposes/blocks (him)". Thus the whole line prays that no one hinder his accession to kingship.
segments.s735-g169: This clause lists three items before the relative clause introduced by که. همان is a demonstrative placed before the noun phrase meaning 'that same' or 'the very'; هدیه is the head noun 'gift'; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking the items; باژ is a noun here (literally 'cry' or fanfare) coordinated in the list; ساوی is another coordinating noun/adjective meaning 'comparable' or 'equal' used as a substantive. The final که begins the relative clause that modifies the whole list.
segments.s735-g170: فرستم is first‑person present/subjunctive form 'I send' (in poetry this carries the sense 'I send' / 'I will send'); به is the preposition 'to/for'; نزدیک is an adverb/adjective 'near' or 'toward'; آن is the demonstrative 'that'; انجمن is the noun 'assembly' or 'gathering'. Word order Verb → prepositional phrase is standard here (poetic prose permits this ordering).
segments.s735-g171: پذیرد is a third‑person optative/subjunctive form meaning 'may he accept' or 'let him accept'; پذیرم is first‑person present/subjunctive 'may I accept' or (here) 'may he accept my acceptance/receive' — in classical/poetic Persian such stacked verbal forms express wishes or petitions. سپاسی is the noun 'a thanks' or 'my thanks' governed by the verbs; ب + دان (بدان) functions adverbially here as 'for that' / 'thereby'.
segments.s735-g172: مبیناد is a subjunctive/optative form of 'to appear/ be seen' (here used negatively in sense 'may it not be seen' or 'may it not appear'); چشم تو is the possessive noun phrase 'your eye' (چشم = eye, تو = you/your); روی is 'face' (object of vision); ب + دان (بدان) again functions adverbially/ demonstratively 'that' or 'thereby'. The verb governs the following noun phrase, with the object following verb as common in Persian poetry.
segments.s735-g173: شود is the third‑person optative/subjunctive 'may it become' or 'let it be'; فرخ is an adjective 'fortunate/auspicious' placed after the verb for poetic effect; این جشن و آیین ما is the noun phrase 'this festival and our ritual' serving as the semantic subject/thing that should become فرخ. In Persian poetry adjectives and verbs often invert relative to prose order.
segments.s735-g174: درخشان is the adjective 'shining/bright' placed before the verb for emphasis; شود again is the optative 'may it become'; در + جهان marks the locative 'in the world'; دین ما is the noun phrase 'our religion' (دین = religion, ما = our). The clause literally reads 'shining may become in the world our religion' — normal poetic inversion with an implied 'it' as subject.
segments.s735-g175: همان is the demonstrative 'that very' or 'the same'; روزه is the noun 'fast' or 'fast‑day'; پاک is the adjective 'pure' modifying روزه; یکشنبدی is an adjective/attributive form 'Sunday's' or 'of Sunday' (i.e., held on Sunday). The phrase names the particular sacred fast being praised.
segments.s735-g176: ز is the preposition 'from' (poetic form of از); هر means 'every'; در is 'door' or 'threshold' (metonymic for house/household); پرستنده is the agent noun 'worshipper' or 'devotee'; ایزدی is the adjective 'divine' modifying پرستنده. The clause omits an explicit verb (a common poetic ellipsis) — literally 'from every door (comes) a divine worshipper.'
segments.s735-g193: ازان نامه is a temporal/causal phrase 'thereupon/from that letter' (classical poetic use). شد is the past-tense copular/causative 'became'; شاه خرم نهان is the subject + predicate where خرم (joyful) is qualified by نهان (adverbial 'secretly/privately'). Word order places the predicate after the subject as is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s735-g194: برو here functions as a temporal/linking particle equivalent to 'thereupon/so' in this poetic line; تازه شد is a compound predicate 'became new/renewed'; روزگار مهان ('the time/days of the guests') is the noun phrase that takes that predicate. The verb شد follows the adjective تازه, forming 'was made new'.
segments.s735-g195: بسی is an adverb meaning 'much/very' (classical usage) modifying آفرین (praise/acclamation). آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction where آفرین (the noun) is 'made' by کرد (to do/make): 'praised a great deal'. برخانگی is a locative/advantage phrase 'in the household/at court' attached to the verb.
segments.s735-g196: بدو is the dative 'to him'; گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here used in its ordinary sense though the listed senses are conversational). بس کن is an imperative formula: بس ('enough/stop') + کن (imperative auxiliary 'do') = 'stop it'. ز is the preposition 'from/of' and بیگانگی is the object 'alienation/estrangement'—so the whole clause is an addressed injunction: 'stop (this) estrangement.'
segments.s735-g197: گرانمایه را is a definite/marked object or beneficiary phrase 'the esteemed/precious one' with را as the definite object marker. جایگه is the noun 'place/seat/position' and ساختند is the past plural verb 'they made/constructed'. The order is object (or beneficiary) + object noun + verb, a normal Persian pattern.
segments.s735-g198: دو ایوان فرخ is a simple noun phrase 'two auspicious/fortunate ivans (porticoes)'. بپرداختند is the past plural verb meaning here 'they attended to / finished / adorned' (in architectural contexts it often means 'completed/laid-out'). Number (دو) precedes the noun as usual.
segments.s735-g199: ببردند is the past plural verb 'they carried/brought'. چیزی is the fronted indefinite 'something' and که introduces a relative clause 'that/which'; بایست is the modal 'must/ought', followed by برد (infinitival/lexical verb 'to take/carry' in classical usage) — together 'چیزی که بایست برد' = 'something that ought to be carried'. The relative clause follows its head (چیزی) as is normal.
segments.s735-g200: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the vicinity of'; آن مرد بیدار is the definite noun phrase 'that awake/alert man' (an epithet). گرد is from گردیدن 'to go/turn' and here functions as the verb 'they went/turned (toward)' or more generally 'moved to' — an archaic/classical verbal form used in narrative.
segments.s735-g177: برو is an imperative 'go' addressing someone or introducing an instruction; سوکواران is the following nominative plural 'the archers' (subject of the verb that follows); بمالند is a present-subjunctive/imperative-like form meaning 'they rub/smear' (classical poetic sequence: command + subordinate verb); روی here is the direct object 'the face'. The sequence literally reads 'Go — (let) the archers rub the face.'
segments.s735-g178: بروبر is an adverbial/particle meaning something like 'all over' or 'right upon' modifying the action that follows; فراوان further intensifies it 'plentifully/abundantly'; بسایند is a present-subjunctive/plural verb from سائیدن 'to rub/abrade, scrape' (here 'scrape away' or 'abrade'); موی is the object 'the hair'. Together: 'let them abundantly scrape/abrade the hair all over.'
segments.s735-g179: شود is a future/passive-leaning verbal auxiliary 'will become/come to be'; آن here points to 'that' or 'then' (temporal demonstrative); زمان‌بر is an adjectival/nominal compound literally 'time-consuming' but used poetically as 'the (appointed) time' or 'that time'; دل ما is the possessor phrase 'our heart(s)'; درست is a predicate adjective 'right/proper' describing the state of our heart(s). The whole segment reads 'that time will come when our heart(s) are made right.'
segments.s735-g180: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/when'; از is the preposition 'from/out of'; کینه دلها places 'hatred' as located in 'the hearts' (دلها = 'the hearts'); بخواهیم is the present-subjunctive/optative 'we may wish / we will want'; شست (here) functions as the object/infinitival idea 'to wash (away)' (archaic/poetic form related to شستن). The clause: 'that we may wish to wash hatred from the hearts.'
segments.s735-g181: که is a relative/linking particle 'that/which'; بود is the past copula 'was/existed'; از گه 'since the time of' is the temporal prepositional phrase (گه = 'time/occasion'); آفریدون is the proper name Afarīdūn; فراز is an adverb/adpositional element 'on high / aloft' modifying the state. The common English order becomes 'which had existed on high since the days of Afarīdūn.'
segments.s735-g182: که again introduces a subordinate clause 'when/which'; با marks accompaniment 'with'; تور and سلم are proper names (Tur and Salm, brothers in the epic); اندر آمد is the compound verbal phrase 'came in / entered' (اندر = 'into/within' + آمد = 'came'); براز is 'dung/manure' — the object that 'came in.' The clause means 'when dung came in with Tur and Salm,' a poetically critical image.
segments.s735-g183: شود is the auxiliary 'will become/shall be'; کشور is the subject 'country/realm'; آسوده is the predicate adjective 'at peace/relieved'; از introduces what the realm will be free of; تاختن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'to raid / to make a cavalry attack' (here 'raiding/charging'). The segment expresses 'the realm will become at peace, freed from raiding.'
segments.s735-g184: به هر گوشه‌ای is a locative phrase 'in/to every corner'; کینه‌ها is the plural object 'hatreds/grudges'; ساختن is an infinitival verb 'to make/create' (here 'to raise/create grudges'). As a whole: 'and from grudges being created in every corner.' Note the infinitival noun-verb cluster parallels the previous infinitive تاختن.
segments.s735-g121: فریدون (subject) + چو (temporal conjunction) begin a subordinate temporal clause 'when Fereydun...'. ایران is the direct object 'Iran' and بایرج is the indirect object/recipient. سپرد is a past simple verb 'entrusted/handed over' (SOV order typical of Persian; the verb closes the clause).
segments.s735-g122: ز روم و ز چین are two ablative prepositional phrases 'from Rome and from China' modifying the action. نام مردی is a noun phrase 'the name/reputation of a man' (نام = fame). ببرد is the classical/poetic verbal form of بردن used here as a third‑person verbal outcome 'he gained/won' — in epic Persian this form conveys the simple past/resultative meaning 'obtained'.
segments.s735-g123: برو here is an archaic contraction equivalent to بر او ('upon him' / 'to him') and marks the person receiving the praise; آفرین is the object 'praise/acclamation' and کرد is the past tense verb 'made/did' — together 'he gave praise to him'. روز نخست is a temporal adverbial 'on the first day' placed after the verb phrase (typical poetic word order).
segments.s735-g124: دلش را is the definite object 'his heart' (دل + possessive ش + object marker را). ز marks separation/ablative 'from', introducing the things removed: کژی و تاری 'crookedness and darkness'. بشست is the past tense 'washed' (بشست is an epic/poetic past form meaning 'he washed/cleansed'). The clause means he purified his heart of these faults.
segments.s735-g125: This is a nominal phrase listing qualities with an implicit copula ('were'): همه 'all' + بی نیازی 'without need / self‑sufficient' and نیک اختری 'well‑starred / fortunate'. Persian often omits the verb in such descriptive lists; the compounds express states or attributes.
segments.s735-g126: A continuation of the attribute list: three coordinated abstract nouns (بزرگی 'greatness', مردی 'manliness / courage', افسونگری 'enchanting charm' or 'fascination'). No verb is present; these nouns further describe the subjects just mentioned.
segments.s735-g127: تو گویی که is a formulaic clause 'you might say / it seems as if' where گویی functions as a modal/quotative verb 'one would say' addressing the addressee. The subordinate clause یزدان شما را سپرد has یزدان as subject, شما را as the object 'you', and سپرد the past verb 'entrusted' — 'that God entrusted you'.
segments.s735-g128: وزان دیگران is a fused prepositional phrase 'and from those others' (و + ز + آن + دیگران), introducing the source. نام مردی ببرد repeats the construction seen above: نام 'reputation/fame' + مردی here 'manliness/courage' and ببرد the poetic/resultative verb 'he gained/won' — so 'he won the renown of manliness from those others.'
segments.s735-g105: همیشه is an adverb 'always' that sets the temporal frame. به دل is a prepositional phrase literally 'to/in the heart' used idiomatically as 'with/in the heart' (here functioning like 'may your heart be...'). شاد و روشن‌روان are postpositive adjectives describing دل (Persian places adjectives after the noun); روشن‌روان is a poetic compound meaning 'bright‑souled/cheerful'. There is no explicit copula verb — the clause is elliptical/optative (a wish), typical in poetry.
segments.s735-g106: Again همیشه 'always' sets the frame. خرد پیر is a noun + adjective ('wisdom' described as 'old' or 'venerable'); دولت جوان is a parallel noun + adjective pair ('sovereignty/realm' described as 'young' or 'vigorous'). The two pairs are linked by و 'and'. Like the previous segment, the verb is omitted and the sense is optative/stative (a wish that wisdom remain venerable and the realm stay youthful).
segments.s735-g107: گران‌مایه is a prefacing adjective 'precious/dear' modifying the noun شاهی 'kingship' (شاهی here = 'kingship/royalty'). کیومرثی is a nisba/adjectival form (‑ی) meaning 'of Keyumarth/Keyumars' and postmodifies شاهی to yield 'the kingship of Keyumarth'.
segments.s735-g108: همان is a demonstrative 'the same/that very' linking this clause to the preceding phrase. پور is an archaic/patriarchal element meaning 'son/descendant' used before a name; هوشنگ is the personal name Hushang. طهمورثی is another nisba 'of Tahmuras' (an adjectival form). The whole segment is appositive and nominal (no copula), giving a patronymic lineage.
segments.s735-g109: This is a coordinate, verbless nominal phrase. پدر بر پدر and پسر بر پسر use the preposition بر 'upon/on' in a repetitive formula to mean 'father after father' / 'generation after generation' and 'son after son' — a poetic way to indicate continuous succession. The conjunction و links the two parallel phrases.
segments.s735-g110: مبادا introduces a negative wish 'may it not be / God forbid'; followed by que/که introducing the subordinate clause. این گوهر is the demonstrative noun 'this jewel (metaphorical for the kingship)'. آید is the present‑subjunctive form of آمدن 'to come' used here in an optative/negative‑wish construction (مبادا که + subjunctive). آید به سر is an idiomatic sequence 'to come to the head' → 'to befall / come to an end', so the phrase means 'may it never happen that this jewel befalls (an end).'
segments.s735-g111: برین is the poetic contraction of بر + این 'upon/for this'. پاک یزدان is a reverential epithet 'the pure God/Yezdān' and functions as the subject. کند is the optative/present of کردن 'to do/make' (here 'may (he) make/bring about'), and آفرین is a noun 'praise/acclamation' or 'commendation' as the object produced. The whole clause is an optative: 'May the pure God bring about praise (for this).' Note the poetic inversion: the prepositional phrase begins the line and the verb follows the divine subject.
segments.s735-g112: بزرگان ملک و بزرگان دین is a coordinated nominal list 'the great ones of the realm and the great ones of religion' (بزرگان = 'great ones/elders/nobles', modified by ملک 'the realm' and دین 'religion'). These are the agents/entities who, by the preceding clause's sense, will offer praise; the structure is a straightforward parallel apposition linked by و.
segments.s735-g161: برآمد is a past/perfective verb meaning “it arose/occurred” and here functions as a subjectless impersonal ‘it happened’. برین is a poetic form equivalent to به این / 'these' (built from ب + ین), so سالیان دراز follows as the time phrase ‘these long years’ (adjective after noun). Word order is verb + temporal phrase for a lofty, poetic cadence.
segments.s735-g162: سزد‌گر is a conditional/qualifying phrase meaning ‘if fitting / if it is proper’. فرستد is a jussive/subjunctive-style present meaning ‘(he) should send / may (he) send’. بما = ‘to us’, شاه = ‘the king’ as agent or topic, and باز = ‘again’. Together they form a protasis: ‘if it be fitting that the king send us again’.
segments.s735-g163: بدین آرزو is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning ‘with this wish / with this hope’ (بدین = به + این in poetic spelling). شهریار جهان immediately follows as the title ‘the sovereign/king of the world’, so the whole segment sets the frame: ‘with this hope, the world‑ruler…’.
segments.s735-g164: ببخشاید here is an optative or jussive third‑person verb ‘may he grant / may he bestow’. از ما marks the beneficiaries ‘to/for us’ (literal ‘from us’ used idiomatically here), and کهان و مهان are honorific plurals meaning ‘the great/elderly’ or ‘chiefs and nobles’ — poetic epithets for the addressees. The clause thus prays that the sovereign grant (the hoped‑for thing) to us, the eminent ones.
segments.s735-g165: ز گیتی is a prepositional phrase ‘from/across the world’. برو بر کنند is a compound verbal idea: برو (go/away) + بر کنند (they make/raise) used idiomatically here to mean ‘they will raise/cried aloud’; کنَند is the 3rd‑person plural present/future. آفرین is an exclamation ‘bravo / praise’ and functions as what they will utter. Overall: ‘they will cry “bravo” throughout the world.’
segments.s735-g166: که here introduces the following clause giving the reason/force: ‘that / for’. بی تو is a standard prepositional phrase ‘without you’. مبادا is a negative jussive ‘may it not be’ or ‘let there not be’. زمان و زمین are the coordinated nouns ‘time and earth’. Together: ‘that without you may there be no time nor earth’ — a hyperbolic formula of indispensability.
segments.s735-g167: بدان is an imperative ‘know (this)’ formed from ب + دان (poetic/imperative usage). من is the explicit subject ‘I’. ز خسرو ’from Khosrow’ indicates source/giver. پذیرم is 1st‑person present/future ‘I accept’ (here: ‘I accept/receive’), and سپاس is the object ‘thanks / gratitude’. The clause states: ‘Know that I accept thanks from Khosrow.’
segments.s735-g168: نیایش کنم is a verb phrase ‘I will make/do prayer’ (کنم is 1st‑person singular present/jussive used for volition). روز و شب ‘day and night’ gives temporal scope. در سه پاس is a locative/instrumental phrase ‘in/through three watches’ (پاس here = watch/guard/watch period). Together: ‘I will pray day and night in three watches.’
segments.s735-g33: This noun phrase lists goods: چه is an exclamatory/iterative particle introducing an item ('what/indeed'); از is the preposition 'from/of'; جامه نرم رومی حریر is a stacked noun phrase where جامه (garment) is modified by نرم (soft), رومی (Roman/Byzantine) and حریر (silk) — the modifiers follow the head in this Persian poetic order.
segments.s735-g34: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' introducing material/contents; در here is the gem-word intended as 'pearls' (the exact gem-sense is not in the provided list); و links items 'and'; زبرجد (peridot) is another gem; یکی is the numeral 'one/a single'; آبگیر is the noun 'pond/basin' — together: 'a single basin of pearls and peridots.'
segments.s735-g35: همان points back to the previously mentioned item ('that/the same'); باژ here denotes the levied tribute/tribute-payment (sense not in the provided list, so marked absent); کشور is the noun 'country/realm'; که introduces a relative clause; تا ... چار بار ('up to/as many as four times') quantifies the extent — the numeral چار precedes بار (times) in the usual Persian order.
segments.s735-g36: ز introduces the material/measure 'in/from (the amount of)'; دینار رومی is the coin specified (dinar, Byzantine/Roman); هزاران هزار is a poetic pleonasm meaning 'thousands upon thousands' (repetition intensifies quantity).
segments.s735-g37: فرستاد is the past tense 'he sent'; the following words give the direct object: چون here functions as a connective ('as/namely' or introducing the specification), followed by مرد رومی چهل (a numerical noun phrase 'forty Roman men') — Persian can place the numeral after the counted noun in poetry, but here the sense is 'he sent forty Roman men.'
segments.s735-g38: This is a locative/relative clause: کجا ('where/wherever') introduces the clause; هر ('each') quantifies چهل ('the forty'); بود is the past-tense copula 'was'; بیدار دل is a two-word adjective phrase 'awake/alert-hearted' qualifying each man — so 'wherever they were, each of the forty was alert of heart.'
segments.s735-g39: گوی here functions impersonally ('it is said' / 'as the saying goes'); پیش رو is a compound meaning 'preceding/above' (پیش 'before', رو 'toward/on' used here to form the adjective); نام او خانگی states the name: نام (name) + او (his) + خانگی (a proper name here, not the literal adjective 'domestic' — that specific name-sense is not in the provided list).
segments.s735-g40: که introduces a relative clause describing that person; همتا 'a match/peer' is negated by نبودش 'there was not (for him)'; به is the preposition 'in/regarding'; فرزانگی is the abstract noun 'wisdom/sagacity' — together: 'who had no equal in wisdom.'
segments.s735-g65: چنین گفت (literally “thus said”) places the verb phrase first in a classical/poetic word order meaning “thus he said.” پس is a temporal/sequence adverb “then.” شاه را uses the direct-object/topic marker را with شاه to indicate the addressee (“to the king” here with گفت). خانگی follows as the nominative/agent (a member of the household or courtier) who utters the words; خانگی is used as a noun (‘one of the household’/court attendant) rather than the ordinary adjective “domestic.”
segments.s735-g66: The initial که introduces the content/exclamatory clause (“that/—who…”). چون تو = “such as you / like you.” The second که is the relative/connector (‘that/who’), and باشد is the classical present-subjunctive/optative form of بودن here meaning “might be / could be.” به marks the abstract domain (“in/with”) and فرزانگی is the abstract noun “wisdom”; together که باشد به فرزانگی = “who could be in wisdom.”
segments.s735-g67: ز here functions as the comparative marker “than.” خورشید is the standard of comparison (“the sun”). بر چرخ (= “on the sky/heaven”) specifies the locus and modifies the image; تابنده‌تر is the comparative adjective “more shining / more resplendent,” placed at the end as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s735-g68: Again ز = “than” sets up the comparison. جان here means “life” (the essential being, i.e., life itself). سخنگوی (literally “speaker” or “one who speaks”) is used adjectivally/qualitatively to point to spoken word or eloquence, and پاینده‌تر is the comparative “more enduring / longer‑lived.” Together the phrase reads as a poetic comparison: “more enduring in (or than) speech/life.”
segments.s735-g69: مبادا is an optative/negative formula meaning “may it not be / lest.” جهان is the subject “the world.” بی‌چنین here means “not such / without such” (i.e. lacking someone like this), and شهریار is “king” or “ruler.” The whole clause is a wish that the world not be left without such a king.
segments.s735-g70: برومند is an adjective meaning “prosperous / flourishing” (here a wish about the times). بادا is the optative particle “may it be.” برو روزگار is a poetic address to “the age/times” (روزگار); برو may be read as part of an idiomatic/poetic phrase invoking the times — together the phrase expresses the wish “may the age be prosperous.”
segments.s735-g71: مبیناد is an optative/subjunctive verbal form meaning roughly “may it not appear / may it not be seen.” کس here functions as “anyone/no one” in the idiomatic optative context, روز = “day,” and بی‌کام = “unfulfilled / unsuccessful.” تو marks the person addressed (“you”). The clause expresses the wish that no one witness a day when you are unlucky or unfulfilled.
segments.s735-g72: نوشته is the noun/participle “written” (used here with an implicit optative: “may it be written”). بخورشید is the poetic locative “on/to the sun” (classical contraction of به خورشید), بر = “on/upon,” نام = “name,” and تو = “your.” The idiom means “may your name be inscribed on the sun” (a high, hyperbolic wish for fame and renown).
segments.s735-g137: ز marks the source or domain ('from/of') in a poetic register; the phrase lists the group and the thing related to them in the order 'ز + group + modifier' (ز ترکان همه) followed by the noun phrase بیشه نارون ('grove of elm'). In other words, literal order: 'from the Turks, all (the) elm‑grove.'
segments.s735-g138: بشستند is a past‑tense plural verb 'they washed' (active); the subject is implied by the narrative context. وبی is the fused coordinating particle + negation ('and without'); رنج here means 'hardship' and is negated by بی; گشت is the past intransitive 'became' and links to انجمن as the predicate complement: 'the assembly became without hardship.' Word order is poetic (object/phrase before the verb).
segments.s735-g139: ز again marks origin/'from' (here 'from the enemy'); برستند is a past plural verb (classical use) meaning roughly 'they were freed/they got rid (of)' or 'they delivered (the world) from'—the exact valency is classical/poetic. چندی follows to indicate duration 'for a while' and modifies the scope, and جهان is the noun 'world' (object/scene) positioned after the temporal adverbial.
segments.s735-g140: برو here is a verbal/metrical particle meaning 'thereupon/then (went up)' or simply 'went' in poetic usage and takes آفرین ('acclamation/praise') as what went up. از introduces the source ('from the') and کهان و مهان is a genitive list 'the ancients and the guests/visitors' — thus: 'acclamations went up from the ancients and the guests.'
segments.s735-g141: ز again marks the group/source and introduces a list of peoples joined by و ('and'): 'ز تازی و هندی و ایرانیان' = 'from (the) Arabs and Indians and Iranians.' The sequence is a simple enumerative noun phrase in classical/poetic order.
segments.s735-g142: ببستند is past plural 'they bound/girded' (here reflexive or reciprocal in sense 'they girded themselves'); پیشش is the prepositional phrase 'before him/in his presence'; کمر بر میان is an idiomatic noun phrase 'waist about the middle' = 'gird the loins' with بر indicating 'around/on' and میان 'the middle'. The object phrase (کمر بر میان) follows the verb in typical Persian order, with پیشش specifying the location/context.
segments.s735-g143: روارو is an adverb/adjective 'face‑to‑face, opposite'; چنین 'thus' modifies the manner; تا ... مرز خزر introduces the extent 'as far as the border of the Khazar(s)'. The sequence places manner first (روارو چنین) and then the extent marker تا plus destination (به مرز خزر).
segments.s735-g144: ز marks the starting point 'from Armenia' (ارمینیه); تا marks the endpoint 'as far as'; در باختر literally 'the gate/entrance of the west' (در used in the sense 'gate') — together this gives the geographic span 'from Armenia to the western gate.' Word order is the typical classical pair 'ز ... تا ...' for range.
segments.s735-g49: برفتند is a 3rd-person plural past tense verb ('they departed/rode off') placed at the start in epic word order. با او is a prepositional phrase 'with him' indicating accompaniment. سواران شاه is the noun phrase 'the king's horsemen' (subject) following the verb, showing the typical Persian order where the verb can precede the subject for narrative effect.
segments.s735-g50: به سر is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'onto the head' (به = toward/onto + سر = head). برنهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of برنهادن, 'they raised/placed on' (here 'put on'). زرین modifies کلاه (adj before noun) to give 'golden hat/helmet' — the whole phrase means 'they put golden helmets on their heads.'
segments.s735-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از دور is a prepositional phrase 'from afar' (از = from + دور = distant). دید is the simple past 3rd-person singular 'he saw' and takes the following noun phrase آن سپه خانگی as its object: 'he saw that host/army (سپه) described by خانگی.' The demonstrative آن marks that particular host.
segments.s735-g52: به پیش literally 'toward the front/presence' (به = to/toward + پیش = in the presence of). اندر آمد is a compound verbal phrase 'came in/advanced' (اندر = in/within used in older/poetic Persian + آمد = came). به بیگانگی is an adverbial phrase 'in estrangement/with a foreign or strange manner' (به marking the manner), describing how he entered.
segments.s735-g53: چنین 'thus/so' sets the manner. تا here means 'until' or 'as far as' and combines with به نزدیک شاه 'to near the king' (به = to, نزدیک = near, شاه = king). آمدند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they came/approached.' The clause states the movement up to a point near the king.
segments.s735-g54: بران is an archaic/adverbial pointer 'thereupon' or 'upon that.' نامور پیشگاه is a noun phrase 'renowned presence/court' (نامور modifying پیشگاه). آمدند repeats the verb 'they came' and puts the action into that renowned court; the verb again appears at the end in standard Persian order.
segments.s735-g55: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. دیدند is 3rd-person plural past 'they saw.' زیبا رخ شاه را is the definite object phrase 'the king's beautiful face' with را marking the definite object (شاه governed by رخ). The object marker را confirms that what follows is the thing perceived.
segments.s735-g56: بران is a linking adverb 'upon that/thereupon' referencing the previous sight. گونه here means 'manner/appearance' and آراسته‌گاه is a poetic compound 'ornamented place/state' — together 'that ornamented aspect/guise.' The final را again marks this phrase as a definite object or emphasized topic tied to what they saw.
segments.s735-g1: به قیصر یکی نامه فرمود شاه is a canonical word order in classical Persian: indirect object ('به قیصر' = 'to/for Caesar') + quantifier/noun ('یکی نامه' = 'one/a letter') + verbal predicate ('فرمود' = 'he said/sent [a letter]') + explicit subject ('شاه' = 'the king'). فرمود is a past/perfective reporting verb; here it introduces reported speech (that-clause follows).
segments.s735-g2: که برنه سزاوار شاهی کلاه is the content of the letter introduced by که ('that'). برنه is an adjective 'bare/naked' modifying an implicit head (lit. 'bare (one)'); سزاوار takes a direct object marked here by noun phrase 'شاهی کلاه' ('the kingly hat/crown'). Note the adjective–predicate order: '[it] is not worthy of the kingly crown' (supply the copula/implicit verb in English).
segments.s735-g3: که مریم پسر زاد زیبا یکی: که again introduces reported content ('that'); the sequence gives the agent/name ('مریم') then the kin-term with birth-root ('پسر زاد' = 'son-born / bore a son') and modifiers ('زیبا یکی' = 'a beautiful one' / 'a handsome one'). Classical Persian often strings noun + زاد to indicate someone born/produced.
segments.s735-g4: که هرگز ندیدی چنو کودکی: a relative/comparative clause introduced by که meaning 'who/which' or continuing the description 'such that...'. هرگز ندیدی is a negative perfect/past ('you have never seen'); classical negative is fused (ن + دیدی). چنو = 'such/as' introduces the comparison, and کودکی ('a child') is the noun being compared.
segments.s735-g5: نشاید مگر دانش و تخت را is built around نشاید, an impersonal/classical negative predicate meaning 'it is not fitting' or 'it should not be'. مگر here functions as 'except'/'unless'. دانش و تخت را — the noun phrase 'knowledge and the throne' carries the object/topic marker را, showing that these are the things judged by the impersonal predicate (i.e. things that should not be granted/held).
segments.s735-g6: وگر در هنر بخشش و بخت را begins the exception clause: وگر = 'but if' / 'unless' and در هنر = 'in/with skill' (هنر = skill). بخشش here carries the sense of giving/generosity (classical vocabulary overlaps with 'grant'), and بخت = fortune/luck. را repeats the object/topic marking — these qualities are the condition making 'knowledge and throne' fitting. The clause thus supplies the proviso for مگر in the previous segment.
segments.s735-g7: چو من شادمانم تو شادان بزی: چو = 'when' (temporal), followed by the first-person clause 'من شادمانم' ('I am joyful' — شادمان + personal ending -م). The second half addresses the addressee: تو شادان + بزی. شادان is adjectival 'joyful'; بزی is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person present/subjunctive/imperative form meaning 'be/live' ('be joyful' or 'live joyfully'). The verb is archaic in form and matches the classical register.
segments.s735-g8: که شاهی و گردنکشی را سزی: که here resumes/carries the causal/explanatory sense 'for'/'because' linking to the previous exhortation. شاهی و گردنکشی را ('kingship and defiance/pride' marked with را as the objects/topic) is followed by سزی, an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'befits / is fitting' (cognate to Persian سَزد / سَزیدن). The lexical entry supplied in the prompt does not list this verbal sense, so that specific sense is represented as unavailable in the provided lexicon.
segments.s735-g185: زن و کودک is a coordinated noun phrase 'women and children'. رومیان follows them and functions as their modifier/possessor (poetic omission of the ezāfe is common), so the phrase reads 'the women and children of the Romans'. برده‌اند is the perfect/past compound of بردن ('have taken/carried off') in 3rd plural; here it expresses that they have been carried off (active or resultative sense) and agrees in number with the understood plural agent.
segments.s735-g186: دل ما = 'our heart' (collective: 'our hearts/the heart of us'). ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'. هر گونه = 'every kind/way' (intensifier: 'in every respect'). آزرده‌اند is the perfect of آزردن ('to distress/hurt'); the plural ending -اند reflects agreement with ما (our collective voice) or a plural reading of دل.
segments.s735-g187: برین is the preposition بر + این ('on/for/by this') contracted in poetry, meaning 'by this' or 'because of this'. خویشی ما = 'our kinship/affinity' (خویشی takes the possessor ما; ezāfe is omitted). جهان رام گشت is a small clause where رام is a predicate adjective 'tame/pacified' and گشت ('became') is the past verb 'became/was made'.
segments.s735-g188: همه کار = 'all work/effort' (subject). بیهوده is a predicative adverb/adjective meaning 'in vain/fruitless'. پدرام is a proper name inserted here; the verb گشت ('became') at the end agrees with the subject همه کار and yields 'all effort became vain' with پدرام appended (address or emphasis using the name in the line).
segments.s735-g189: درود here = 'blessings/greetings'. جهان‌آفرین is a compound 'world-creator' (creator of the world). بر تو باد is a fixed optative formula: بر = 'upon', تو = 'you', باد = optative particle 'may it be'. The whole clause is a short wish: 'May the Creator's blessing be upon you.'
segments.s735-g190: همان = 'the same'. آفرین here functions as a deverbal noun/stem referring to the 'Creator' (lit. 'one who creates'), so همان آفرین زمین = 'that same Creator of the earth'. بر تو باد repeats the optative 'may ... be upon you', forming a parallel blessing to the previous half-line.
segments.s735-g191: چو = 'when' (subordinating conjunction). آن نامه قیصر = 'that letter of/from Caesar' (noun phrase). آمد = past 'arrived/came'. ببن is an imperative/archaic form of 'ببین' used as an interjection 'look/see' or 'behold', here introducing the reaction that follows.
segments.s735-g192: جهاندار = 'the sovereign/ruler' (subject). بشنید is the past form 'he heard' (third person). چندان modifies سخن and means 'so much/that many' or 'such (words)'; سخن = 'speech/words'. Together: 'the sovereign heard such words' or 'heard so many words.'
segments.s735-g57: نهادند is a past‑tense 3rd plural verb ('they placed/laid'); here it governs the following object phrase. همواره is an adverb ('always/ever') placed after the verb for emphasis. سر بر زمین is the object phrase ('head upon the ground'), with the preposition بر + noun زمین giving the direction/location.
segments.s735-g58: This clause describes a repeated/outburst action. همی‌خواندند is the imperfective/habitual past ('they were saying/they would shout') marked by the older prefix همی‑. آفرین is an interjection/acclamation. The preceding برو بر functions poetically as an emphatic/repetitive adverbial (a short fixed phrasing in classical usage meaning 'again and again' / 'one after another'), so the whole clause means they repeatedly cried out “Afarin.”
segments.s735-g59: بمالید is a past 3rd plural verb ('they rubbed/pressed'); پس is a temporal/sequence adverb ('then'). خانگی here means 'of the household/attendants' (i.e. the household people) and modifies رخ ('face'); بخاک is a direction phrase ('to the earth/ground'), so together they rubbed/pressed their faces to the ground.
segments.s735-g60: همی‌گفت is the habitual/imperfective past ('they kept saying' / 'they would say'), introducing reported vocative speech. کای is an archaic vocative particle 'O' placed before the addressee; داور is the addressee ('judge/arbiter'). داد و پاک are epithets in apposition ('justice' / 'just' and 'pure'), forming the content of the repeated invocation.
segments.s735-g61: ز is a poetic preposition 'from/on account of' marking the source ('from the conqueror'). آفرین بر تو باد is a fixed optative formula: آفرین ('praise' / exclamation) + بر تو ('upon you') + باد (optative particle 'may it be'). The whole gives the wish 'May the conqueror's praise be upon you.'
segments.s735-g62: مبادی is plural 'beginnings/origins' or 'sources' used here as the thing wished for; همیشه ('always') is its adverb. مگر is specifying/limiting in a classical poetic sense ('namely / particularly' / 'rather'), introducing the standard/model that follows (شاه و راد = 'the king and the noble'). Altogether it expresses the wish that one’s beginnings/standing be ever like those of kings and nobles.
segments.s735-g63: بزرگانش ('his great ones') is the subject (nobles, eminent men); از جای is a prepositional phrase ('from their place/seats'), and برخاستند is the past 3rd plural verb ('they rose up' / 'they stood'). Word order is subject + prepositional phrase + verb, typical in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s735-g64: به نزدیک ('to/near') marks movement toward the king (شه). جایش (jây + ِش) is 'his place/seat' (the king's place), with the enclitic ش = 'his'. آراستند is a past 3rd plural verb ('they adorned/arranged'), so the clause says they came close to the king and set/arranged his place.
segments.s735-g41: همی‌شد is an imperfective/habitual past marker meaning ‘it used to happen’ (an archaic, poetic past). برین گونه is the fused poetic form of بر این گونه = ‘in this manner’ (بر + این), and با ساروان (‘with the saravān’) attaches as a prepositional phrase: ‘with the caravan‑drivers/leaders’. Word order (verb — adverbial phrase — prepositional phrase) is typical of old/poetic Persian where the manner phrase follows the verb.
segments.s735-g42: This noun phrase lists the cargo and the head noun: شتربار (‘camel‑load/cargo’) qualifies the following material (دینار), then the numeral ده (‘ten’) quantifies دینار, and کاروان is the head noun ‘caravan’. The phrase therefore describes ‘caravans whose camel‑loads were dinars, ten (per...)’ — in natural English, ‘caravans loaded with ten dinars (per camel)’. The order (qualifier — material — numeral — head noun) is a compact poetic stacking.
segments.s735-g43: چوآگاهی آمد is a temporal subordinate clause: چو (when/as) + آگاهی (news/awareness) + آمد (came) = ‘when news arrived’. The verb آمد is simple past, and به + پرویز شاه is a dative phrase ‘to King Perviz’ (the recipient of the news). This is a normal past temporal construction in classical Persian.
segments.s735-g44: که introduces the content of the news (a reported clause): ‘that …’. پیغمبر قیصر here functions as ‘the messenger/agent of Caesar’ (in older usage پیغمبر can mean ‘envoy’ or ‘messenger’ in context), آمد is past ‘came’, and ز راه is a poetic form of از راه = ‘from/along the road’. So the whole clause renders the substance of the tidings.
segments.s735-g45: به فرخ بفرمود is ‘he said/ordered to Farrokh’ — بفرمود is an honorific/polite past used for a lord’s speech (‘he commanded/said’). تا here introduces a purpose/content clause (‘so that’ / ‘that he should…’) and is followed by the verb برنشست (past form ‘sat up / took a seat’). The sequence expresses an order with a subordinate clause of result or purpose.
segments.s735-g46: یکی is the indefinite ‘a certain/someone’, serving as subject introducer; مرزبان is the noun ‘border‑governor’ (marzban). بود is the past copula ‘was’, and خسروپرست is a post‑nominal epithet ‘one who worships/worshipper of the Khosrow’ i.e. a king‑worshipping sycophant. The whole short clause means ‘there was a certain marzban, a sycophant of the king.’
segments.s735-g47: که is the relative pronoun ‘who/that’ linking back to ‘a certain marzban’. سالار او بود is a simple nominal clause: سالار = ‘commander/chief’, او = ‘his’ (the king’s), بود = past copula ‘was’; بر نیمروز means ‘over Nimruz’ (a regional jurisdiction). The sequence describes that this marzban held the office of commander in Nimruz.
segments.s735-g48: گرانمایه گردی و گیتی فروز are epithets/appositions naming or praising him: گرانمایه (‘esteemed/precious’) modifies گردی (here functioning as a name or title element), و = ‘and’, گیتی فروز = ‘world‑illuminating’ or ‘that which makes the world bright’ (an honorific epithet). These are poetic honorifics placed after the noun to describe the marzban’s status and fame.
segments.s735-g25: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the material; the noun phrase دیبای زربفت رومی names the cloth (literally 'Rūmī gold-woven dibā/brocade'). The numeral دویست (two hundred) follows the noun phrase in this poetic word order, so the whole means 'two hundred pieces of Rūmī gold-brocade.'
segments.s735-g26: که introduces a reported/subordinate clause 'which/that'; گفتی here functions as the reported-speech verb 'you said' (an archaic, poetic past form). ز زر جامه = 'a garment of gold' (ز used poetically = 'of/with [gold]'), با = 'with', رز here means a rosy hue or 'rose-colored' and یکیست = 'is one / is the same' (i.e. 'is equal to'). The clause therefore reports what 'you said': that a gold robe is equal to one with a rose hue.
segments.s735-g27: چهل خوان is 'forty khwān' (خوان = banquet, table, feast) where the numeral precedes or follows in poetic usage; زرین modifies خوان as 'golden'. پایه بسد is a verb phrase: پایه = 'foundation/base' and بسد (from بسیدن) = 'suffice' or 'is enough'. The whole clause says these forty golden banquets/tables serve as a sufficient basis.
segments.s735-g28: چنان کز (چنان که + کز) is a poetic connective 'so that/so... that', introducing the result clause. در here means 'door/gate' (not the preposition), شهر یاران = 'the city of the companions/friends', and سزد is an impersonal/present form 'it befits' / 'it suits'. So: 'so that it is fitting for the gate of the friends' city.'
segments.s735-g29: همان چند = 'those same several' (demonstrative + quantifier); زرین و سیمین = 'golden and silvery' (paired adjectives describing the items). دده here is ambiguous/archaic in form: in this context it reads as a vocative or addressee ('O Dede' / 'Dede'), addressing the person who will deal with the gifts; the line-order is poetic and omits an explicit verb that is supplied by the next segment's verb phrase.
segments.s735-g30: بگوهر is a verb meaning 'set with gems' (ب‑ as the verbal prefix + گهر 'gem'); بر = 'upon/on'; و = 'and'; چشمشان = 'their eyes' (چشم + possessive ‑شان); آژده = 'startled/astounded' used predicatively. Together: 'set (them) with gems upon (them), and their eyes were startled.' Note the object of بگوهر is understood from the previous segment.
segments.s735-g31: بمریم is the fused prepositional phrase 'به مریم' = 'to Maryam' (the spelling without space is common in older/poetic texts). فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent' (3rd person singular). چندی گهر = 'some jewels' (چندی = some/several; گهر = jewel) is the direct object. Word order is verb + object after the prepositional indirect object, typical in narrative.
segments.s735-g32: یکی = 'one' (indefinite/ numeral), نره = 'male' (specifying sex of the animal), طاوس = 'peacock', کرده is the participial/past form 'made' or 'having made/arrayed' and here functions to qualify the peacock as 'having been made/arranged (grandly)', and بزر is an adjective 'great/large'. Together this short nominal phrase lists 'one male peacock, made great' — i.e. a magnificent male peacock was among the gifts.
segments.s735-g81: برفتیم is the past first‑person plural (بر + رفتیم) meaning 'we went/set forth.' The preposition با + noun (با فیلسوفان) follows the verb here as a prepositional object 'with the philosophers.' بهم is an adverb meaning 'together' modifying the motion.
segments.s735-g82: بران is an adverbial/locative 'thereupon'/'thither' linking to the preceding action. تا introduces a purposive/subjunctive clause ('so that'); نباشد is the negative subjunctive/jussive form 'may not be'/'let there be no' (archaic/poetic negative). کس is the indefinite 'anyone' (here negated by نباشد), از ما marks 'from/among us', and دژم is a predicate adjective 'sullen/gloomy' following the noun.
segments.s735-g83: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (ز قیصر = 'from the Caesar'). پذیرد is an archaic third‑person singular present/future sense 'he accepts/receives.' مگر after the verb functions as 'except,' and باژ و چیز are the items excepted (direct objects). Word order places the verb phrase before the exception phrase for emphasis.
segments.s735-g84: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause modifying the preceding noun(s). با + باژ 'with cry' links an instrumental phrase, و چیز adds 'and thing/matter.' آفرینست is an archaic third‑person singular predicate (here functioning as 'is made/commended/admired' in poetic usage) and نیز 'also' reinforces that the same applies to these things.
segments.s735-g85: بخندید is past third‑person singular 'he laughed' and with از + NP (از آن) means 'laughed at that.' پر هنر is a compound adjective 'full of skill' placed before مرد ('man') as an epithet; شاه (king) is the agent who laughs—poetic word order puts the verb early for effect.
segments.s735-g86: نهادند is the past plural verb 'they placed/set' (نهاد + -ند). زرین یکی is a noun phrase 'a golden one' (زرین modifying یکی), where یکی can mean 'one/a certain [thing].' پیشگاه is locative 'before the presence/at the court' indicating where the item was placed.
segments.s735-g87: فرستاد is past third‑person singular 'he sent.' پس is an adverb 'then' (sequencing the action). چیزها is the plural direct object 'the things/goods.' سوی + گنج is a directional phrase 'toward the treasury/treasure‑house' (گنج here as the repository).
segments.s735-g88: بدو is an indirect object 'to him.' گفت here is used as the past tense verb 'he said' (the lemma senses list includes noun senses, but context shows verbal use). چندین is a quantifier 'many/several.' نبایست is the negative modal 'ought not / must not / should not' (archaic form), and رنج is the noun 'suffering/hardship' governed by the modal.
segments.s735-g73: This is a nominal clause headed by جهان (the world) followed by a predicate phrase 'بی‌سر و افسر تو' and the optative negator مباد. بی‌سر is a prefixed adjective (bi‑ + sar) meaning 'without a head/leader' and comes before the noun it modifies in effect; افسر 'helmet' follows and is linked to تو (your) as possessive. مباد is an optative/negative particle meaning 'may it not be' and applies to the whole clause.
segments.s735-g74: Parallel clause structured like the previous: 'بر و بوم' (literally 'roof and soil' = the land/realm) is the noun phrase, followed by the preposition-like element بی 'without' plus لشکر 'army' and finally the optative negative مباد. Word order is NP + predicate + optative; مباد negates the wish.
segments.s735-g75: Two coordinated prepositional/elegiac phrases introduced by ز 'from': 'ز قیصر درود' = 'a greeting from the Caesar' and 'ز ما آفرین' = 'from us, acclamation/bravo'. آفرین is used here as an exclamatory noun ('bravo'/'acclamation'), and the coordination is straightforward.
segments.s735-g76: This is a short noun phrase headed by برین (بر + این = 'to/for this') where بر functions here toward/for and -ین is the orthographic form of 'این' ('this'), followed by نامور شهریار زمین 'renowned prince/king of the land' as the addressee. The phrase as a whole marks the recipient of the greetings.
segments.s735-g77: Relative clause introduced by کسی 'someone/anyone' + the archaic relative/cohesive particle کو (here functioning like 'who/that' — listed senses didn't include this use, see senses mapping). درین is a folded prepositional item (در + این = 'in this'), so درین سایه شاه = 'in this king's shadow/protection'. شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy/joyful' that completes the clause.
segments.s735-g78: This clause contains the negative optative/existential verb نباشد ('may there not be' / 'let there be no') targeting the person mentioned, followed by ورا 'to/for him' and روشنایی 'light, brightness'. مباد again is the optative negative ('may it not be'), so the whole line expresses the wish that no one be deprived of joy under the king and that light not be absent from him.
segments.s735-g79: The speaker's action: آمدم 'I came' is the finite verb. The phrase before it lists accompaniment/means: هدیه 'gifts' and باژ 'cry/clamour' from روم 'Rome'. The initial word ابا is archaic/uncertain in this context (not matching the provided senses for refusal) and here functions like an idiomatic introducer meaning roughly 'bearing/with' (hence rendered 'with gifts'); its exact etymology is archaic.
segments.s735-g80: Parallel clause: برین = بر + این ('to/into this'), where بر means 'toward/to' and -ین is the orthographic form of 'این' ('this'); نامبردار بوم 'renowned/native land' is the goal, and آمدم again is the first‑person past 'I came'. The second repetition of آمدم emphasizes arrival at the named place.
segments.s735-g17: بدرگاه + بردند + چندی + صلیب: بدرگاه is a locative/dative phrase 'to/into the court' placed before the verb (a common poetic ordering). بردند is the simple past, 3rd‑person plural 'they carried/took'. چندی here is an adverb 'for a while' modifying the verb phrase. صلیب is the direct object 'the cross'; the whole clause means 'they carried the cross into the court for a while', with the time/extent (چندی) placed near the verb for emphasis.
segments.s735-g18: نسیم گلان آمد و بوی طیب: نسیم گلان is a poetic noun‑noun (genitive) phrase 'a breeze of (the) flowers' where the linking ezāfe is omitted; آمد is 3rd‑sg. past 'came'. The conjunction و connects a coordinated verbless clause 'بوی طیب' (literally 'a pleasant scent') — the verb (آمد) is understood to apply to both coordinated nouns, so 'a breeze of flowers came and (a) pleasant perfume (came/was wafted)'.
segments.s735-g19: بیک هفته زین گونه با رود و می: بیک هفته is an archaic temporal phrase 'for one week' (بیک here functions as an old numeral/adverbial); زین گونه = 'in this way / thus' (an adverbial phrase). با introduces accompaniment; رود here is the musical instrument (rūd, lute/viol) rather than a river, and می is 'wine'. Word order is adverbial phrase → accompaniment; the verb that governs them appears in the next segment.
segments.s735-g20: ببودند شادان ز شیروی کی: ببودند is a past/optative form meaning 'they would have been' (a potential/conditional past sense). شادان is the predicate adjective 'joyful'. ز is causal 'from/because of'. شیروی is an epithet 'lion‑like, brave' modifying the following element; کی here appears as part of a name/epithet (not the interrogative) and so functions as a proper-name element. The clause reads 'they would have been joyful because of the lion‑like Kay', with ببودند giving a counterfactual or potential tone.
segments.s735-g21: بهشتم بفرمود تا کاروان: بهشتم is a dative/indirect object 'to Behishtam' (name or title placed before the verb). بفرمود is the polite/honorific past 3rd‑person 'he ordered/said' (بفرمود is more formal than گفت). تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result 'so that / that'. کاروان is the subject of that subordinate clause 'the caravan'.
segments.s735-g22: بیامد بدرگاه با ساروان: بیامد is the 3rd‑sg. past 'came'. The subject (کاروان) is understood from the previous clause. بدرگاه is the place adjunct 'to the court' following the verb (poetic ordering). با ساروان 'with the caravan‑leaders' indicates accompaniment; ساروان here means the leaders/drivers of the caravan. The clause thus completes the content of the order: 'that the caravan come to the court with its leaders.'
segments.s735-g23: صد اشتر ز گنج درم بار کرد: صد + noun is the numeral phrase 'a hundred [camels]'. اشتر (here 'camels') appears as the counted noun (the listed lemma is also a personal name in the lexicon, so the precise sense is signalled below). ز گنج 'from the treasury' is a source phrase, درم is the money (dirhams) used as the load, بار کرد is a light‑verb construction 'loaded (with) / made a load of'. The verb کرد is 3rd‑sg. past and takes the preceding nominal cluster as its object/compliment.
segments.s735-g24: چو پنجه شتر بار دینار کرد: چو is a circumstantial connector 'as / when' introducing the parallel/example clause. پنجه شتر here is a small numeric/grouping phrase 'five camels' (پنجه is an older/counting word in context) and thus the noun phrase is the object of the following light verb. بار دینار کرد = 'he loaded (them) with dinars' (دینار is the cargo, کرد is past 3rd‑sg.). The clause repeats the 'loaded camels with money' idea but with a different quantity/type of coin.
segments.s735-g129: A nominal phrase of epithets: هنر پرور is a compound adjective meaning “fosterer/nurturer of art” (هنر = art/skill + پرور = nurturing). The coordinate adjectives راد و بخشنده further describe the same person: راد is an epithet ‘generous / noble’, and بخشنده here takes the following noun (گنج) as its object: “giver of treasure.” Word order is noun/adjective compounds and coordinated adjectives modifying the same referent.
segments.s735-g130: A prepositional + negative clause giving the claim about lineage: ازین (از + این, “from this”) marks origin (‘from this seed/line’). تخمه = offspring/stock. هرگز is the adverb “never.” نبد is an archaic past/coprular negative (comparable to نبـود/نشد) meaning “there was not / did not happen.” کس here means “anyone/no one” with the negation. به رنج = ‘to/into suffering’ — together: “from this stock no one ever came to suffering.”
segments.s735-g131: نهادند is a past plural transitive verb “they set/placed/put (upon).” بر دشمنان is the prepositional object “upon/against the enemies.” باژ و ساو are coordinated nouns indicating noise/clamour (onomatopoetic words here: “cries and tumult”) and function adverbially/with instrumental force. The phrase as a whole means “they set (attacked) the enemies with cries/clamour.”
segments.s735-g132: This is the continuing result clause tied to نهادند: بد اندیشتان is a descriptive group meaning “the ill‑thinking ones / those with bad intent” (an adjectival compound). بارکش is a noun/participial label “load‑bearer” (one who bears a load) and serves as the predicate role they were made to assume; همچو گاو is a simile “like a cow,” modifying بارکش. In other words, “(they) made the ill‑minded bear loads like cattle.” The verb is supplied from the previous clause (نهادند).
segments.s735-g133: A temporal noun phrase: ز هنگام = a poetic prepositional expression ‘since/from the time of’; کسری is the proper name Kisra (Khosrow). نوشین روان is a two‑word epithet: نوشین (‘pleasant/delightful’) modifies روان (‘soul/spirit’) and together mean “the sweet‑souled” or “pleasant of spirit.” The whole functions as a temporal/epithetical reference: “from the time of Khosrow, the sweet‑souled (one).”
segments.s735-g134: An optative clause introduced by که … بادا: که here introduces the wished clause and بادا is the optative particle ‘may it be’ (a fixed poetic formula). همیشه is the adverb ‘always,’ روانش is روان + possessive suffix -ش ‘his soul,’ and جوان is the predicate adjective ‘young / vigorous.’ Together: “may his soul always be young.”
segments.s735-g135: A relative clause opened by که ‘who/that.’ از ژرف دریا is the prepositional phrase ‘from the deep sea’ (ژرف = deep, دریا = sea). برآورد is a past verb meaning ‘he brought up / raised / caused to appear’ (here ‘raised’ or ‘drew up’), and پی (literally ‘foot/track’) is used in the architectural sense ‘foundation/footing’ (i.e., the base). So: “who raised a foundation from the deep sea.”
segments.s735-g136: Continuation of the relative idea: بران گونه = ‘in that manner / thereby’ (بران = thereon/thence, گونه = manner), دیوار بیدار = ‘a wakeful/alert wall’ (دیوار = wall, بیدار = awake/alert) — i.e., a vigilant or watchful barrier — and کی here is a final interrogative/emphatic particle (‘who? / indeed who’) used rhetorically. Together the line says he set up a vigilant wall in that fashion.
